perturbation.original_text: Prior to disclosing proprie tary or CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION of the PROVIDER, the RECIPIENT shall first not ify and provide it an opportunity to prevent disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Prior to disclosing proprie tary or CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION of the PROVIDER, the RECIPIENT shall first do nothing to notify and provide it an opportunity to prevent disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for the recipient to notify the provider before disclosing confidential information, effectively negating any opportunity for the provider to prevent such disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the general principles of trade secret protection embodied in the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which implies a duty to protect confidential information. By explicitly stating that no notification is required, the agreement undermines reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, potentially violating the spirit of the law. MD Code, Commercial Law, § 11-1203 deals with injunctive relief for misappropriation. By removing the notification requirement before disclosing confidential information, the disclosure could then be considered misappropriation if done improperly.

(a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. (b) Damages under this subtitle may include: (1) The actual loss caused by misappropriation; and (2) The unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. (c) In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (d) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a) of this section. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the spirit of trade secret protection, specifically regarding the duty to protect confidential information, as the perturbation explanation points out. The original text required notification prior to disclosure, allowing the provider to potentially prevent it. The changed text explicitly negates this notification requirement. This negation undermines the reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, potentially leading to misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both referencing MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1203, discuss damages for misappropriation. By removing the notification requirement, the disclosure could be considered misappropriation if improperly done, supporting a determination that a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 4. RECIPIENT‘s obligations under Paragraphs 2 and 3 above shall not extend to any part of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: (a) that can be demonstrated to have been in the public domain or publicly known and readily av ailable to the trade or the public prior to the date of the disclosure; or (b) that can be demonstrated, from written records, to have been in RECIPIENT's possession or readily available to RECIPIENT from another source not under obligation of secrecy to PROVIDER prior to the disclosure; or (c) that becomes part of the public domain or publicly known by publication or otherwise, not due to any unauthorized act by RECIPIENT; or (d) that is subsequently disclosed to RECIPIENT by a third party who is not under an obligation of confidentiality to PROVIDER; or (e) that is required by any law, regulation, or order of court to be disclosed by RECIPIENT . Prior to disclosing proprie tary or CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION of the PROVIDER, the RECIPIENT shall first not ify and provide it an opportunity to prevent disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Neither party shall use the name of the other or any contraction or derivative thereof or the name(s) of the o ther party's faculty members, employees, or students, as applicable, in any advertising, promotional, sales literature, or fundraising documents without prior written consent from the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the exceptions regarding what constitutes confidential information and the duty to notify the provider, makes it difficult to enforce the confidentiality provisions, as the definition of what information is protected is no longer clear, as the sections that define it are completely removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: By obfuscating the definition of what constitutes a trade secret within the agreement, specifically moving section 4 which clarifies exceptions to confidentiality, the modified contract creates ambiguity that undermines the enforceability of trade secret protections under the MUTSA. This is problematic because the MUTSA requires a clear identification of what constitutes a trade secret. The contract is no longer clear and undermines protections, potentially leading to a failure to meet the statute's requirements for enforceability. § 11-1201(e) defines 'Trade Secret'.

(a) In this subtitle the following words have the meanings indicated. (b) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (c) “Misappropriation” means the: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the person’s knowledge of the trade secret was: 1. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; 2. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of the person’s position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (d) “Person” means an individual, corporation, business trust, statutory trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (e) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (1) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (2) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. perturbation.justification: The changed text, by removing the exceptions to the confidentiality obligations (section 4 in the original text), creates a structural flaw that undermines the enforceability of trade secret protections, which directly relates to the definition of trade secrets under the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA). By moving section 4, the contract's clarity regarding what constitutes a trade secret is compromised. The Law Explanation specifically mentions that 'MUTSA requires a clear identification of what constitutes a trade secret,' and Scraped Law Snippet 1 (and 2) §11-1201(e) defines 'Trade secret' as information that derives economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. Removing exceptions makes it unclear what is protected, potentially making it harder to prove something qualifies as a trade secret, therefore leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. RECIPIENT's obligations under Paragraphs 2 and 3 shall extend for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. RECIPIENT's obligations under Paragraphs 2 and 3 shall extend for a period of one (1) year from the effective date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period from 5 years to 1 year significantly reduces the protection afforded to the disclosing party's confidential information, which might be unreasonably short depending on the nature of the information and the industry. This timeframe may be deemed inadequate for certain types of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the MUTSA does not explicitly mandate a minimum duration for confidentiality agreements, shortening the protection period to one year could be deemed unreasonable given the nature of certain trade secrets (e.g., complex technologies or long-term strategic plans). A court might find the reduced timeframe insufficient to provide adequate protection, especially if the information requires a longer period to maintain its competitive advantage. The appropriateness of the duration is typically considered when evaluating if reasonable efforts were made to maintain secrecy under § 11-1202(a).

(a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. (b) Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (c) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. (d) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (e) In this section, “exceptional circumstances” includes a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, which may contradict the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy under the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA). While MUTSA doesn't specify a minimum duration, the Law Explanation argues a one-year period might be deemed unreasonable for certain trade secrets, especially if a longer period is needed to maintain a competitive advantage. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both quoting Maryland Commercial Law Code § 11-1202(a), state that "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." Reducing the protection period weakens the ability to enjoin misappropriation, as a one-year period may be insufficient for adequate protection. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction' definition, where the change affects the clarity and enforceability of the contract regarding trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: 6. It is understood that nothing herein shall be deemed to constitute, by implication or otherwise, the grant to RECIPIENT of any license or other rights under any patent, patent application, or other intellectual property right or interest belonging to PROVIDER, or as permitting RECIPIENT to unfairly obtain the right to use any CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION which becomes publicly known through an improper act or omission on its part.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. It is understood that nothing herein shall be deemed to constitute, by implication or otherwise, the grant to RECIPIENT of any license or other rights under any patent, patent application, or other intellectual property right or interest belonging to PROVIDER, or as permitting RECIPIENT to unfairly obtain the right to use any CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION which becomes publicly known through an improper act or omission on the PROVIDER's part.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'its part' to 'PROVIDER's part' shifts the responsibility for confidential information becoming publicly known from the recipient to the provider. This weakens the protection for the provider, as it now implies that if the provider is responsible for the information becoming public, the recipient is free to use it.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the intent of MUTSA, which aims to protect trade secrets when reasonable efforts are made to maintain secrecy. By stating that the RECIPIENT can unfairly obtain the right to use CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION if the PROVIDER causes it to become public, the agreement weakens the confidentiality protection. The MUTSA generally considers misappropriation even if the information becomes public through improper means, and it expects parties to take reasonable measures to safeguard trade secrets under § 11-1202(a). Placing blame on the Provider reduces liability to the RECIPIENT.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA) by shifting responsibility for the confidentiality of information from the recipient to the provider. As the Perturbation explanation describes, by shifting responsibility to the provider, the recipient is free to use the confidential information. This contradicts the intent of MUTSA to protect trade secrets when reasonable efforts are made to maintain secrecy. According to scraped law snippet 1 and 2 (MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1202 (2022)), "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined". The modification weakens the protection for the provider and suggests that the recipient can unfairly use confidential information if the provider causes it to become public, thus contradicting the core principle of MUTSA.
